name: Essential Chinese
description: Mandarin from absolute zero, structured the way spaced-repetition research says to learn a language: the tone and pinyin system first, then day-one survival phrases, then the highest-frequency words, then the grammar patterns that snap them together. Characters ride up front with tap-to-hear speech, pinyin and usage notes appear on every reveal, and the phrases you must be able to say are trained in both directions. Simplified characters. Keep new cards to 5-10 a day and say every answer out loud.

fields.front: 妈 mā, 麻 má, 马 mǎ, 骂 mà: same letters, four different words. What tells them apart?
fields.back: The tone: the pitch pattern of the syllable. It is part of the word itself, not decoration.
fields.extra: mā mom, má hemp, mǎ horse, mà to scold. Mandarin has four tones plus a neutral tone; every pinyin syllable in this deck carries its tone mark.

fields.front: Tone 1, the flat bar: mā. How does it sound?
fields.back: High and level, like a sung note held steady.
fields.extra: Think of a doctor's 'say aaah'. Example: 妈 mā (mom).

fields.front: Tone 2, the rising accent: má. How does it sound?
fields.back: Rises from mid to high, like the end of an English question: What?
fields.extra: Example: 茶 chá (tea).

fields.front: Tone 3, the dipping accent: mǎ. How does it sound?
fields.back: Low and slow: it sinks to the bottom of your voice, often creaky, and only rises in careful speech.
fields.extra: Example: 水 shuǐ (water). Think low, not down-then-up.

fields.front: Tone 4, the falling accent: mà. How does it sound?
fields.back: Falls sharply from high to low, like a curt English 'No!'
fields.extra: Example: 是 shì (to be).

fields.front: The neutral tone: the second ma in māma. How does it sound?
fields.back: Short, light, unstressed; it takes its pitch from the syllable before it.
fields.extra: Written with no tone mark. Example: 谢谢 xièxie, second syllable.

fields.front: 你好 is written nǐ hǎo, two 3rd tones in a row. How is it actually pronounced?
fields.back: ní hǎo: when two 3rd tones meet, the first turns into a 2nd (rising) tone.
fields.extra: Automatic everywhere: 很好 hěn hǎo comes out hén hǎo. Pinyin keeps writing the original tones; your mouth makes the switch.

fields.front: Pinyin q, as in qǐng: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like English 'ch' but with lips spread wide and the tongue tip down behind the lower teeth.
fields.extra: 请 qǐng (please) sounds close to 'ching'. Never like the q in 'queen'.

fields.front: Pinyin x, as in xièxie: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like English 'sh' but with lips spread in a smile and the tongue tip down.
fields.extra: 谢谢 xièxie (thank you) sounds close to 'shyeh-shyeh'.

fields.front: Pinyin zh, as in zhè: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like the 'j' in judge, with the tongue curled back.
fields.extra: 这 zhè (this) sounds close to 'juh'.

fields.front: Pinyin c, as in cèsuǒ: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like the 'ts' in cats, at the start of the syllable.
fields.extra: 厕所 cèsuǒ (toilet) sounds close to 'tsuh-swaw'. Never like English k or s.

fields.front: Pinyin z, as in zàijiàn: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like the 'ds' in kids: a quick 'dz'.
fields.extra: 再见 zàijiàn (goodbye) sounds close to 'dzai-jyen'.

fields.front: Pinyin r, as in rè: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Between an English 'r' and the 's' in measure, with no lip rounding.
fields.extra: 热 rè (hot). Keep your lips relaxed; English r rounds them.

fields.front: Pinyin ü, as in nǚ: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Say 'ee', hold your tongue there, and round your lips.
fields.extra: 女 nǚ (female). After j, q, x, y it is written as plain u but still sounds like ü: 去 qù.

fields.front: Pinyin i after z, c, s, zh, ch, sh, r (as in sì and shí): how does it sound?
fields.back: Not 'ee': the vowel is a buzzed continuation of the consonant.
fields.extra: 四 sì (four) is close to 'szz', 十 shí (ten) to 'shrr'. Everywhere else i is 'ee': 你 nǐ.
**nǐ hǎo**, said ní hǎo (two 3rd tones). 你 you + 好 good. Works with anyone, any time of day.
**nǐ hǎo ma**. 吗 turns a statement into a yes-no question. Standard reply: 我很好.
**wǒ hěn hǎo**. 很 is required glue between a subject and an adjective; here it barely means 'very'.
**xièxie**, second syllable light and toneless. Add the person: 谢谢你.
**bú kèqi**. Literally 'don't be polite'. 不 shifts to 2nd tone before a 4th tone.
**duìbuqǐ**. The apology for real faults: bumping into someone, being late.

fields.back: excuse me, sorry to bother you
fields.extra: **bù hǎoyìsi**. The everyday soft apology: squeezing past someone, interrupting, asking a favor.

fields.back: it doesn't matter (reply to an apology)
fields.extra: **méi guānxi**. The standard answer to 对不起. Literally 'there is no connection'.
**zàijiàn**. 再 (again) + 见 (see): 'see you again'.
**qǐng**. Starts polite requests: 请坐 (please sit). Also 'to treat': 我请你 (my treat).
**qǐngwèn**. The polite opener before any question to a stranger.
**nǐ jiào shénme míngzi**. Answer with 我叫 wǒ jiào + your name.
**rènshi nǐ hěn gāoxìng**. Literally 'knowing you, (I'm) very glad'.
**wǒ shì Měiguó rén**. Country + 人 (person) = nationality. Swap in any country.
**wǒ tīng bu dǒng**. Literally 'I hear but can't understand'. The most useful sentence of your first month.
**qǐng zài shuō yí biàn**. 再 (again) + 说 (say) + 一遍 (once through).
**qǐng shuō màn yìdiǎn**. Adjective + 一点 means 'a bit more so'.
**nǐ huì shuō Yīngyǔ ma**. 会 marks a learned ability. 英语 English language.
**wǒ huì shuō yìdiǎnr Hànyǔ**. Guaranteed smiles. 一点儿 a little.
**zhè shì shénme**. Point and ask. Question words sit where the answer would go: 'this is what?'
**duōshao qián**. 多少 (how much) + 钱 (money). Point first: 这个多少钱？
**tài guì le**. The 太...了 wrapper means 'too/so'. Your opening move in any market haggle.
**piányi yìdiǎn**. The follow-up to 太贵了. 便宜 cheap + 一点 a bit.
**wǒ yào zhège**. Point at the menu and say it; works in any shop or restaurant.

fields.back: I don't want it, no thanks
fields.extra: **wǒ bú yào**. Firm but not rude; perfect for street vendors.
**cèsuǒ zài nǎr**. X 在哪儿 finds anything. Politer word for toilet: 卫生间 wèishēngjiān.
**fúwùyuán**. Call it out to get attention in a restaurant; normal, not rude. Literally 'service person'.
**mǎidān**. Say it to the server when you want to pay.
**gānbēi**. Literally 'dry the cup'. At formal toasts it can mean bottoms-up, so pace yourself.
**děng yíxià**. 等 (wait) + 一下 (a little); 一下 softens any verb.
**méi wèntí**. The easygoing yes to any request. 问题 problem, question.
**jiùmìng**. 救 (save) + 命 (life). For everyday help say 请帮我 qǐng bāng wǒ.
**wǒ**. Add 的 for 'my': 我的.
**nǐ**. A polite form 您 nín exists, but 你 is fine in nearly every situation.
**tā**. 他 (he), 她 (she), and 它 (it) all sound identical; only the writing differs.
**tā**. Same sound as 他; the woman radical 女 marks 'she' in writing only.
**wǒmen**. 们 makes pronouns plural: 你们 (you all), 他们 (they).
**rén**. A picture of a walking person. Country + 人 = nationality: 中国人.
**péngyou**. 男朋友 boyfriend and 女朋友 girlfriend add 男 (male) or 女 (female).
**zhè**. Takes a measure word before nouns: 这个 zhège (this one).
**nà**. 那个 nàge (that one). Don't confuse with 哪 nǎ (which), which has an extra mouth 口.
**líng**. Fills empty slots when reading numbers aloud: room 302 reads sān líng èr.
**yī** when counting. In phrases the tone shifts: yí before 4th tones (一下), yì before the rest (一点).
**èr**. For counting and math. Two OF something uses 两 liǎng instead: 两个人.
**sān**. 三点 (3 o'clock), 三个人 (three people).
**sì**. Unlucky: sounds like 死 sǐ (death); buildings often skip the 4th floor. Mind sì (4) vs shí (10).
**wǔ**. 五块钱 (five yuan).
**liù**. A lucky number; 666 in Chinese chat means 'awesome', nothing sinister.
**qī**. 七点 (7 o'clock).
**bā**. The lucky number: it sounds like 发 fā (to get rich). Plates full of 8s cost extra.
**jiǔ**. Sounds like 久 jiǔ (long-lasting), so nine is auspicious for anniversaries.
**shí**, with the buzzed vowel: 'shrr'. sì (4) falls, shí (10) rises.
**bǎi**. 一百 (100), 三百 (300). 105 is 一百零五, with 零 filling the empty tens slot.
**qián**. 多少钱？ (how much?). The metal radical 钅 recalls coins.

fields.back: two of something (before a measure word)
fields.extra: **liǎng**. 两个人 (two people), never 二个人. 二 counts in the abstract; 两 counts things.

fields.text: Numbers 11 to 19 stack ten + digit: 11 is {{c1::十一}}, 14 is {{c2::十四}}.
fields.extra: shíyī, shísì. The same pattern runs through 十九 (19).

fields.text: Tens stack digit + ten: 20 is {{c1::二十}}, 99 is {{c2::九十九}}.
fields.extra: èrshí, jiǔshíjiǔ. Mix freely: 25 is 二十五 èrshíwǔ.

fields.front: 一 yī changes tone in phrases. What is the rule?
fields.back: yí before a 4th tone (一下 yíxià), yì before any other tone (一点 yìdiǎn); plain yī when counting.
fields.extra: Same spirit as 不 turning into bú before 4th tones. Pinyin in this deck writes the spoken tone.
**shénme**. Sits where the answer would go: 这是什么？ ('this is what?')
**shéi** (formal shuí). 他是谁？ (Who is he?)
**nǎr**. 你在哪儿？ (Where are you?). The South and Taiwan say 哪里 nǎlǐ instead.
**duōshao**. Literally 'many-few'. No measure word needed: 多少钱？

fields.back: how is it? how about it?
fields.extra: **zěnmeyàng**. The all-purpose 'what do you think': 这个怎么样？ (How about this one?)
**nǎ**. 哪个 (which one), 你是哪国人？ (Which country are you from?). It is 那 (that) plus a mouth 口 asking the question.
**jǐ**. For answers under about ten, always with a measure word: 几个人？ Also 几点？ (what time?)
**zěnme**. 怎么说？ (How do you say it?), 怎么去？ (How do I get there?)
**ma**, toneless. Statement + 吗 = question: 你是老师吗？ Word order never changes.
**ne**. Returns a question: 我很好，你呢？ (I'm fine, and you?)
**shì**. Links nouns only: 我是学生 (I am a student). Never before adjectives: 我很好, not 我是好.
**yǒu**. Negated with 没, never 不: 没有. 有问题吗？ (Any questions?)
**yào**. 我要这个 (I want this one). Direct; for 'would like to do' use 想.

fields.back: to want to (do something); to miss
fields.extra: **xiǎng**. 想 + verb: 我想去 (I'd like to go), softer than 要. With a person: 我想你 (I miss you).
**qù**. 去 + place: 去中国 (go to China).
**lái**. Also 'bring me' when ordering: 来一杯茶 (a cup of tea, please).
**chī**. 吃饭 (have a meal). 你吃了吗？ (Have you eaten?) doubles as a friendly greeting.
**hē**. 喝水 (drink water), 喝茶 (drink tea). The mouth radical 口 marks a mouth action.
**mǎi**. 买东西 (go shopping). 卖 mài (to sell) differs by tone and a top stroke: 3rd tone buys, 4th tone sells.
**kàn**. 看书 (read a book), 看电影 (watch a movie). A hand 手 shading an eye 目.
**tīng**. 听不懂 (hear but not understand) is the one you will use most.
**shuō**. 说汉语 (speak Chinese). 说话 means to talk in general.
**huì**. 我会说汉语 (I can speak Chinese). Compare 能 néng: can in the moment.
**néng**. Ability or permission in the moment: 我不能去 (I can't go). Learned skills take 会.
**zài**. 我在家 (I'm at home). Before a verb it marks an ongoing action: 我在吃饭 (I'm eating).
**zhù**. 你住在哪儿？ (Where do you live?). Hotels too: 住饭店.
**méiyǒu**. Negates 有 and completed actions: 我没有去 (I didn't go). Often shortened to 没.
**zuò**. 做饭 (cook). Same sound as 坐 (to sit); context decides.
**zuò**. 请坐 (please sit). 坐飞机 (take a plane), 坐出租车 (take a taxi). Two people 人 sitting on earth 土.
**jiào**. 我叫 + your name (My name is...). Also to summon: 叫出租车 (call a taxi).
**xuéxí**. 我学习汉语 (I study Chinese). 学 alone works too.
**gōngzuò**. Both verb and noun: 我在中国工作 (I work in China); 你的工作 (your job).
**shuìjiào**. 我要睡觉 (I want to sleep). Not 水饺 shuǐjiǎo (boiled dumplings); tones matter.
**dǎ diànhuà**. 打 (to hit) is the verb for calls: 给我打电话 (call me). 电话 is 'electric speech'.
**ài**. 我爱你 (I love you). Strong; for everyday liking use 喜欢.
**xǐhuan**. 我喜欢中国菜 (I like Chinese food). Takes nouns or verbs: 喜欢看书.
**jīntiān**. 天 (day) builds the whole trio: 今天, 明天, 昨天.
**míngtiān**. 明 (bright: sun 日 + moon 月). 明天见 (see you tomorrow).
**zuótiān**. 昨 carries the sun radical 日: a day already passed.
**xiànzài**. 现在几点？ (What time is it now?)
**xiànzài jǐ diǎn**. Answer: number + 点: 三点 (3 o'clock).
**diǎn**. 三点 (3 o'clock). Also 'to order food' (点菜) and 'a little' (一点).
**nián**. Dates run big to small: 2026年7月9日. 新年 (New Year).
**yuè**. Months are just numbers: 一月 (January) through 十二月 (December). The character draws a crescent moon.
**xīngqī**. Weekdays count up: 星期一 (Monday) to 星期六 (Saturday); Sunday is 星期天.
**shàngwǔ**. 上 (upper) + 午 (noon). The afternoon is 下午.
**xiàwǔ**. 下 (lower) + 午 (noon): the part of the day below noon.
**bàba**. Second syllable is neutral tone.
**māma**. Woman radical 女 plus 马 mǎ (horse) lending the sound.
**érzi**. 子 here is a neutral-tone noun suffix, the same one as in 杯子 (cup).
**nǚ'ér**. 女 (female) + 儿 (child). The apostrophe separates the syllables; ü keeps its dots after n.
**lǎoshī**. 老 (old, venerable) + 师 (master). Address teachers as surname + 老师: 王老师.
**xuésheng**. 学 (to study) + 生 (person). 我是学生 (I am a student).
**yīshēng**. 医 (medicine) + 生 (person). 医院 (hospital) shares the same 医.
**xiānsheng**. Surname first: 王先生 (Mr. Wang). Literally 'born earlier'.
**xiǎojiě**. Fine after a surname: 王小姐. Alone to a stranger it can offend; say 服务员 to staff instead.
**shuǐ**. 热水 (hot water) is the default drink offered in China.
**chá**. Both 'chai' and 'cha' around the world come from this word.
**kāfēi**. A loanword from 'coffee'. 一杯咖啡 (a cup of coffee).
**mǐfàn**. 米 (raw rice) + 饭 (cooked food, meal). 吃饭 (have a meal) uses the same 饭.
**cài**. 中国菜 (Chinese food), 点菜 (order dishes). The grass radical 艹 hints at its vegetable origin.
**shuǐguǒ**. 水 (water) + 果 (fruit): juicy produce.
**píngguǒ**. Also the brand: 苹果手机 (iPhone).
**bēizi**. 杯 doubles as the measure word for drinks. Same 杯 as in 干杯 (cheers).
**jiā**. A pig 豕 under a roof 宀: the ancient picture of a household. 回家 (go home).
**Zhōngguó**. 中 (middle) + 国 (country): the Middle Kingdom. 中国人, 中国菜.
**Měiguó**. 美 (beautiful) + 国 (country), picked to echo the 'me' in America. 美国人 (American).
**Hànyǔ**. 汉 (the Han people) + 语 (language). You will also hear 中文 Zhōngwén; both are fine.
**chūzūchē**. 出租 (to rent out) + 车 (vehicle). 坐出租车 (take a taxi); in speech also 打车 dǎchē.
**shǒujī**. 手 (hand) + 机 (machine). 我的手机 (my phone).
**xuéxiào**. 学 (study) + 校 (school building). 去学校 (go to school).
**fàndiàn**. 饭 (meal) + 店 (shop). In big cities a 饭店 is often a hotel; a small eatery is a 饭馆.
**shāngdiàn**. 商 (commerce) + 店 (shop). 去商店买东西 (go to the store to buy things).
**yīyuàn**. 医 (medicine) + 院 (institution).
**shū**. 看书 (read), 一本书 (one book).
**dōngxi**. Literally 'east-west'. 买东西 (buy things, go shopping).
**fēijī**. 飞 (to fly) + 机 (machine). The airport, 机场 jīchǎng, shares the 机.
**diànnǎo**. Literally 'electric brain'. 电 also starts 电话 (phone) and 电影 (movie).
**yīfu**. 买衣服 (buy clothes).
**gǒu**. Animal radical 犭 on the left. 小狗 (puppy).
**qiánmiàn**. 饭店在前面 (the restaurant is up ahead).
**hòumiàn**. 在学校后面 (behind the school). Opposite of 前面.
**lǐ**. Follows the noun: 家里 (at home), 学校里 (inside the school).
**hǎo**. Woman 女 + child 子 = good. Also an agreeable 'OK' on its own: 好的.
**dà**. A person with arms stretched wide. 大学 (university, literally 'big school').
**xiǎo**. 小狗 (puppy). 小 + surname is a friendly address for someone younger: 小王.
**guì**. 太贵了 (too expensive).
**piányi**. 便宜一点 (a little cheaper). Opposite of 贵.
**duō**. 很多 (a lot). Opposite: 少 shǎo (few). 多少 (how much) stacks them.
**lěng**. 今天很冷 (it's cold today). The two-stroke ice radical 冫 sits on the left.
**rè**. 热水 (hot water). The four dots at the bottom are the fire radical.
**gāoxìng**. 高 (high) + 兴 (spirits). 认识你很高兴 (nice to meet you).
**piàoliang**. The liang is a neutral tone. Works for people, clothes, and cities alike.
**de**, toneless. 我的手机 (my phone), 你的书 (your book). The most common character in Chinese.
**le**. After a verb: completed (我买了 I bought it). At sentence end: new situation (下雨了 it's raining now).
**hěn**. Required between subject and adjective: 他很好. Often so weak it just means 'is'.
**bù**. The all-purpose negator: 不是, 不去. Shifts to bú before 4th tones: 不是 bú shì. 有 takes 没 instead.
**tài**. The 太...了 pattern: 太贵了 (too expensive), 太好了 (great!).
**dōu**. Goes after the subject, before the verb: 我们都去 (we are all going).
**hé**. 我和你 (you and I). It cannot join sentences the way English 'and' does.
**yìdiǎnr**. 一点儿水 (a little water). After adjectives: 便宜一点儿 (a bit cheaper).
**yí ge rén**. 个 gè is the default measure word. Number + measure word + noun is mandatory: never 一人.
**yì běn shū**. 本 běn is the measure word for books and notebooks.
**yì bēi chá**. 杯 bēi measures drinks: 一杯咖啡, 一杯水.
**shí kuài qián**. 块 kuài is the spoken unit of money (the formal word is 元 yuán); 钱 often drops off: 十块.
**wǒ sānshí suì**. Age needs no verb. 岁 suì (years old). Ask a child 你几岁？, an adult 你多大？

fields.text: Ask a yes-no question: 你去{{c1::吗}}？(Are you going?)
fields.extra: nǐ qù ma. 吗 turns any statement into a question; the word order never changes.

fields.text: Bounce a question back: 我很好，你{{c1::呢}}？(I'm fine, and you?)
fields.extra: wǒ hěn hǎo, nǐ ne.

fields.text: Make it yours: 我{{c1::的}}手机 (my phone)
fields.extra: wǒ de shǒujī. 的 links owner to thing: 你的书 (your book).

fields.text: Say no: 我{{c1::不}}去 (I'm not going)
fields.extra: wǒ bú qù. 不 negates most verbs and adjectives.

fields.text: The exception: 我{{c1::没}}有钱 (I don't have money)
fields.extra: wǒ méiyǒu qián. 有 is always negated with 没, never 不.

fields.text: Adjective glue: 他{{c1::很}}好 (He is fine)
fields.extra: tā hěn hǎo. Subject + 很 + adjective; no 是.
tài hǎo le. The same frame as 太贵了 (too expensive).

fields.text: All of us: 我们{{c1::都}}去 (We are all going)
fields.extra: wǒmen dōu qù. 都 sits after the subject, before the verb.

fields.text: You and me: 我{{c1::和}}你 (me and you)
fields.extra: wǒ hé nǐ. 和 joins nouns only.

fields.text: Say where you are: 我{{c1::在}}家 (I'm at home)
fields.extra: wǒ zài jiā. 在 + place; no extra 'is' needed.

fields.text: Mark it done: 我买{{c1::了}} (I bought it)
fields.extra: wǒ mǎi le. 了 after the verb marks a completed action.

fields.text: Count with a measure word: 三{{c1::个}}人 (three people)
fields.extra: sān ge rén. Number + measure word + noun, always.

fields.text: Learned skills: 我{{c1::会}}说汉语 (I can speak Chinese)
fields.extra: wǒ huì shuō Hànyǔ. In-the-moment ability would take 能 instead.

fields.text: Find anything: 医院在{{c1::哪儿}}？(Where is the hospital?)
fields.extra: yīyuàn zài nǎr. Swap in any place: 厕所在哪儿？

fields.text: Order anything: 我{{c1::要}}一杯咖啡 (I want a cup of coffee)
fields.extra: wǒ yào yì bēi kāfēi.
